message.content:
You are verifier #1, independent. Do NOT optimistically confirm. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and decide if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in CURRENT code (4 hardening passes applied). Confirmed=true ONLY if the problem exists as described, the PoC reproduces, and it is genuine (not speculative/already-handled/by-design). Cite file:line.

H-011: Graph gate node warns-but-allows a missing trail recorder while run_study's gate path always records (asymmetric audit guarantee)
File: src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py:90-161
Description: Verified: in run_study the gate handler always records to the trail before enforcing (pipeline.py run_gate). The LangGraph graph path make_gate_node (gatenode.py:90-161) makes the trail recorder OPTIONAL: with recorder=None it merely warns (lines 114-115) and records only into non-tamper-evident LangGraph state. The docstring itself (lines 108-112) calls this a 'GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap' where a checkpoint edit flipping rejected->approved is undetectable by verify_records. The same governance concern is structurally guaranteed on one path and merely warned-about on the other.
PoC: make_gate_node(gate, propose) with no recorder builds successfully (only a warning). Operator approvals then live solely in LangGraph checkpoint state; verify_records cannot detect a checkpoint tamper that flips the decision.
Suggested fix: Make the recorder mandatory on make_gate_node (raise when None), matching run_study's unconditional trail.record — or, if a recorder-less node must remain supported, gate it behind an explicit opt-out flag (allow_unrecorded=True) so shipping the audit-gap configuration is a conscious choice.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	"""Human-in-the-loop gate node — the interrupt/resume protocol (GOVERNANCE.md).
2	
3	:func:`make_gate_node` turns an engine :class:`~assay_engine.orchestration.gates.Gate` plus a
4	proposal builder into a LangGraph node that pauses the run at the gate (``interrupt``),
5	surfaces a proposal to the operator, and on resume validates and records the decision.
6	
7	The **gate-correlation guard** rejects a resumed decision aimed at a *different* gate than the
8	one currently parked — a stale or misrouted decision (e.g. delivered late through the
9	governance channel) must not be applied to whatever gate happens to be waiting. The guard's
10	expected gate is the gate the operator named in the resume value, compared against this node's
11	gate; auto-deriving it from the parked gate would make the guard always match and thus a no-op.
12	
13	Two further hardened behaviors, ported faithfully from the prior platform's gate node:
14	
15	- **Re-prompt on a bad resume (not raise).** ``interrupt()`` consumes and *persists* the
16	  resume value into the checkpoint *before* the node returns, so raising on a malformed or
17	  mis-correlated value would strand it in the checkpoint and replay it forever — the gate
18	  would be permanently bricked. Instead the node validates inside a loop and re-fires
19	  ``interrupt()`` (carrying the error) on a bad value, so the operator can re-submit and
20	  actually recover.
21	- **Terminal-decision idempotency.** An ``approved`` decision is terminal: re-entering an
22	  already-approved gate is a no-op (it does not re-prompt or re-record). ``rejected`` and
23	  ``deferred`` are deliberately NOT terminal, so a reject→revise→re-review loop re-fires the
24	  interrupt as intended.
25	
26	The node returns a partial state update appending one record to ``gate_decisions``; a study's
27	graph state should declare ``gate_decisions`` with a list/add reducer so records accumulate
28	across gates. The decision/recording validator (:func:`apply_gate_decision`) is pure and
29	tested offline; only the ``interrupt`` call requires LangGraph (the optional ``orchestration``
30	extra, imported lazily).
31	"""
32	
33	from __future__ import annotations
34	
35	import warnings
36	from typing import Any, Callable, Mapping
37	
38	from assay_engine.orchestration.gates import Gate, GateError
39	
40	GateProposal = Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], Mapping[str, Any]]
41	
42	_VALID_DECISIONS = frozenset({"approved", "rejected", "deferred"})
43	
44	
45	def apply_gate_decision(gate: Gate, resumed: Any) -> dict[str, Any]:
46	    """Validate a resumed operator decision and return the state update to record it.
47	
48	    Raises :class:`GateError` if the resume value is malformed, targets a different gate
49	    (correlation guard), or carries an unrecognized decision.
50	    """
51	    if not isinstance(resumed, Mapping):
52	        raise GateError(
53	            f"gate {gate.name!r}: resume value must be a mapping, got {type(resumed).__name__}"
54	        )
55	    target = resumed.get("gate_id")
56	    if target != gate.name:
57	        raise GateError(
58	            f"gate {gate.name!r}: resumed decision targets {target!r} — stale or misrouted; "
59	            "rejecting (gate-correlation guard)"
60	        )
61	    decision = str(resumed.get("decision", "")).strip().lower()
62	    if decision not in _VALID_DECISIONS:
63	        raise GateError(
64	            f"gate {gate.name!r}: invalid decision {decision!r} "
65	            f"(expected one of {sorted(_VALID_DECISIONS)})"
66	        )
67	    # A rejection/deferral with no rationale is governance-relevant; record a sentinel rather
68	    # than a blank (incl. whitespace-only) so the audit trail is never silently empty.
69	    rationale = str(resumed.get("rationale") or "").strip() or "No rationale provided."
70	    record = {"gate": gate.name, "decision": decision, "rationale": rationale}
71	    return {"gate_decisions": [record]}
72	
73	
74	# Only an approval is terminal: a re-entered approved gate is a no-op, but a rejected/deferred
75	# gate must re-fire its interrupt so a revise→re-review loop can re-prompt the operator.
76	_TERMINAL_DECISIONS = frozenset({"approved"})
77	
78	
79	def already_decided(state: Mapping[str, Any], gate_name: str) -> bool:
80	    """True if ``gate_name`` already holds a terminal (approved) decision in ``gate_decisions``."""
81	    for record in state.get("gate_decisions", []) or []:
82	        if record.get("gate") == gate_name and record.get("decision") in _TERMINAL_DECISIONS:
83	            return True
84	    return False
85	
86	
87	GateRecorder = Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], None]
88	
89	
90	def make_gate_node(
91	    gate: Gate,
92	    propose: GateProposal,
93	    *,
94	    recorder: GateRecorder | None = None,
95	) -> Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], dict[str, Any]]:
96	    """Build a LangGraph node that parks at ``gate`` for an operator decision.
97	
98	    ``propose(state)`` returns the proposal payload surfaced to the operator (what the gate is
99	    asking them to approve). The node returns a ``{"gate_decisions": [record]}`` update, or
100	    ``{}`` (no-op) if the gate is already terminally approved.
101	
102	    ``recorder`` (optional) bridges each validated decision into the hash-chained provenance
103	    trail (#111): the durable LangGraph path otherwise records gate decisions only as
104	    (untamper-evident) graph state, so the documented append-only trail would miss them. Pass a
105	    callback such as ``lambda r: trail.record("gate", f"gate {r['gate']}: {r['decision']}", **r)``
106	    so the graph path's approvals/rejections land in the same chained trail as the runner's.
107	
108	    Omitting ``recorder`` is a GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap: gate decisions then live only in
109	    (non-tamper-evident) LangGraph state, never in the hash-chained trail, so a checkpoint edit
110	    flipping ``rejected``→``approved`` is undetectable by ``verify_records``. Because that
111	    insecure configuration is effortless to ship by accident, building a node without a recorder
112	    emits a warning rather than failing silently (pass 3, #F-007).
113	    """
114	    if recorder is None:
115	        warnings.warn(
116	            f"make_gate_node({gate.name!r}) built with no recorder: gate decisions will NOT be "
117	            "written to the hash-chained provenance trail (GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap) — pass a "
118	            "recorder that calls trail.record('gate', ...) for a tamper-evident audit trail",
119	            stacklevel=2,
120	        )
121	
122	    def node(state: Mapping[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
123	        if already_decided(state, gate.name):
124	            return {}  # terminal decision exists — do not re-prompt or re-record
125	        try:
126	            from langgraph.types import interrupt
127	        except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra
128	            raise RuntimeError(
129	                "orchestration requires the 'orchestration' extra (langgraph) — not installed"
130	            ) from exc
131	        proposal = dict(propose(state))
132	        current: dict[str, Any] = {"gate_id": gate.name, "proposal": proposal}
133	        # Validate inside a loop: on a bad/mis-correlated resume, re-fire interrupt (next
134	        # resume index) carrying the error rather than raising — a raise would strand the
135	        # bad value in the checkpoint and replay it forever, bricking the gate.
136	        while True:
137	            resumed = interrupt(current)
138	            try:
139	                update = apply_gate_decision(gate, resumed)
140	            except GateError as exc:
141	                current = {"gate_id": gate.name, "proposal": proposal, "error": str(exc)}
142	                continue
143	            if recorder is not None:
144	                # Record to the trail BEFORE returning the state update (#G-020): the validated
145	                # decision must land in the hash-chained trail and the graph state atomically. If
146	                # the recorder fails, surface a clear GateError INSTEAD of returning the update —
147	                # so the decision is neither half-applied (in graph state but absent from the
148	                # tamper-evident trail) nor silently lost; the operator/runner sees an explicit
149	                # failure to durably record the approval rather than a quietly diverging trail.
150	                try:
151	                    for rec in update.get("gate_decisions", []):
152	                        recorder(rec)
153	                except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 - normalize any recorder backend failure
154	                    raise GateError(
155	                        f"gate {gate.name!r}: decision validated but could NOT be recorded to the "
156	                        f"provenance trail ({type(exc).__name__}: {exc}); not applying the state "
157	                        "update so graph state cannot diverge from the trail"
158	                    ) from exc
159	            return update
160	
161	    return node
162

content.text: The code matches the description. Now let me verify the asymmetry claim by checking run_study's gate path.

326	    def run_gate(review: GateReview) -> None:

content.text:
Confirmed. The asymmetry is real and exists in current code:

- `run_study`'s `run_gate` (pipeline.py:326-342) **unconditionally** calls `trail.record(...)` before enforcing — no way to bypass the tamper-evident trail.
- `make_gate_node` (gatenode.py:90-161) makes `recorder` an optional parameter (default `None`, line 94). With `None`, it only emits a `warnings.warn` (lines 114-120) and the node body proceeds with no trail recording (lines 143 guard skips). Decisions then live only in LangGraph checkpoint state.

The PoC reproduces: `make_gate_node(gate, propose)` with no recorder builds successfully (only a warning), and at runtime records nothing to the hash-chained trail. A checkpoint edit flipping `rejected`→`approved` would be undetectable by `verify_records`.

Now assessing severity. This is a genuine governance audit-gap asymmetry. However, I must weigh the mitigating factors against the "production-readiness problem" framing:

1. The warning is explicit and documented (pass 3, #F-007 already addressed this by adding the warning).
2. This is the LangGraph orchestration path behind an optional `orchestration` extra — not the default `run_study` path.
3. Building without a recorder is a deliberate caller choice; the warning fires loudly.
4. It is not a vulnerability exploitable by an external actor — it requires the operator to both (a) ship a recorder-less node and (b) have checkpoint-tamper access.

The finding is accurate (true positive, not speculative), but the previous hardening pass deliberately chose warn-over-raise. The suggested fix (mandatory recorder or explicit `allow_unrecorded` opt-out) is a reasonable hardening. The asymmetric audit guarantee is a legitimate medium-severity governance concern: the insecure config is shippable by accident despite the warning, and warnings are easily suppressed/unseen in production.

input.reasoning: Verified against current code. The asymmetry is real and exists as described. run_study's gate path (src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:326-342, run_gate) unconditionally calls trail.record(...) before enforcing the decision — the tamper-evident hash-chained trail is structurally guaranteed. The LangGraph graph path make_gate_node (src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py:90-161) declares recorder as optional (default None, line 94); when None it merely emits warnings.warn (lines 114-120) and the node body skips trail recording entirely (the recorder-is-not-None guard at line 143). The docstring itself (lines 108-112) labels this a 'GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap' where a checkpoint edit flipping rejected->approved is undetectable by verify_records. PoC reproduces exactly: make_gate_node(gate, propose) with no recorder builds successfully (warning only) and records operator decisions solely into non-tamper-evident LangGraph checkpoint state. The same governance invariant is thus structurally enforced on one path and merely warned-about on the other. Not speculative, not by-design (the docstring itself flags it as a gap), and not fully handled — pass 3 (#F-007) added only a warning, which is suppressible and easily unseen in production, leaving the insecure configuration shippable by accident. Severity is medium rather than high: it sits behind the optional 'orchestration' extra (not the default run_study path), requires the operator to both ship a recorder-less node and have checkpoint-tamper access, and a loud warning is emitted. The suggested fix (mandatory recorder, or explicit allow_unrecorded opt-out) is appropriate to make shipping the audit gap a conscious choice rather than an accidental default.

content.text:
Verified and confirmed H-011 as a true positive at medium severity.

Key evidence:
- `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:326-342` — `run_gate` unconditionally records to the hash-chained trail before enforcing.
- `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py:90-161` — `make_gate_node` makes `recorder` optional (line 94), only warns when `None` (lines 114-120), and skips trail recording in the node body (guard at line 143). The docstring itself (lines 108-112) labels this a GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap.

The asymmetry is genuine: one path structurally guarantees the tamper-evident trail, the other merely warns. Downgraded from high to medium because it sits behind the optional orchestration extra, requires both a recorder-less config and checkpoint-tamper access, and a loud warning already fires (pass 3 #F-007). The suggested fix (mandatory recorder or explicit `allow_unrecorded` opt-out) is the right hardening.
